title: Average Stock Market Return: S&P 500 Historical Data 1928...
description: Discover the historical average stock market return. See S&P 500 average returns by 1Y, 5Y, 10Y, 20Y, and 30Y periods, adjusted for inflation and dividends.

schemas.headline: Average Stock Market Return: S&P 500 Historical Data
schemas.description: Discover the historical average stock market return. See S&P 500 average returns by 1Y, 5Y, 10Y, 20Y, and 30Y periods, adjusted for inflation and dividends.

mainEntity.name: What is the average stock market return?

acceptedAnswer.text: Historically, the average stock market return (measured by the S&P 500) has been roughly 10% per year before inflation, assuming dividends are reinvested. When adjusted for inflation (real return), the historical average is closer to 7% per year.

mainEntity.name: What is the average S&P 500 return over 10 years?

acceptedAnswer.text: Over most 10-year rolling periods, the S&P 500 has delivered positive returns. Historically, the annualized nominal return over a 10-year period typically ranges from 8% to 12%, depending on the starting valuation and economic conditions.

mainEntity.name: Does the average stock market return include dividends?

acceptedAnswer.text: When financial professionals cite the 10% historical average return of the S&P 500, they are generally referring to the 'total return,' which includes both price appreciation and the reinvestment of dividends. Without dividends, the average price return is lower.

mainEntity.name: What is the inflation-adjusted (real) stock market return?

acceptedAnswer.text: The inflation-adjusted, or 'real', stock market return accounts for the loss of purchasing power over time. Historically, while the nominal return of the S&P 500 is about 10%, the real return averages roughly 6.5% to 7% per year.

mainEntity.name: What are the best and worst years in stock market history?

acceptedAnswer.text: The best year for the S&P 500 was 1933 with a nearly 50% return following the Great Depression. The worst year was 1931, when the index lost over 43%. More recently, the 2008 financial crisis saw a drop of roughly 37%.
